scope: Homepage conversion architecture + CTA hierarchy + section ordering

executiveSummary.primaryProblem: The page has two legitimate conversion paths (quiz and newsletter) but no clear hierarchy between them. The result is four competing CTAs across nine sections, which research consistently shows reduces conversion rate versus a single dominant CTA with strategic repetition.
executiveSummary.secondaryProblem: The Destinations section (section 7) is structurally out of place. It appears after Authority and Social Proof, when a visitor is maximally primed to convert — and then pivots them into travel inspiration. This is the highest-cost placement for an off-topic section.
executiveSummary.verdict: The bones are strong. The hero copy, problem framing, and social proof are all doing real work. The issue is architectural, not copywriting. Fix the CTA hierarchy and move Destinations — do not rewrite what is already working.
executiveSummary.estimatedConversionLift: 20-40% signup increase from CTA consolidation alone, based on industry A/B test data (pages with 1 CTA convert at 13.5% vs 11.9% with 2-4 CTAs)

sections.name: Hero
sections.assessment: Strong. The headline is differentiated and problem-aware. The CTA is correctly above the fold. The subtext '3 short emails. 5 min each.' is good friction reduction. Keep as-is.

sections.name: Problem
sections.assessment: Excellent. Agitates the problem without pushing a solution yet. The three mistake cards are specific and credible. The '$3,000 surprise' card is the strongest — leads with a concrete number. Keep as-is.

sections.name: Quiz CTA
sections.assessment: This is the structural conflict point. Placed immediately after the Problem section, it hijacks the momentum that should flow into the email guide. The copy 'Let us cut through the noise' is generic and weaker than the hero headline. 'No signup required' undercuts the newsletter path by implying the quiz delivers value for free without an email.
sections.issue: CRITICAL: This section introduces a second primary CTA before the newsletter CTA has been reinforced. It creates a fork in the buyer journey with no clear signal about which path is better for the visitor.

sections.name: Guide Preview (Three emails)
sections.assessment: The strongest newsletter section on the page. The numbered email breakdown is the best explainer of what the visitor gets. This is where the second newsletter CTA belongs and earns its place. Keep.
sections.issue: Minor: Placed after the Quiz CTA, so visitors who clicked away to the quiz never see this section.

sections.name: Authority
sections.assessment: Well-placed. Comes after value delivery (Guide Preview) and before social proof. The 'No skin in the game' card is the strongest trust signal on the page. Keep.

sections.name: Social Proof
sections.assessment: Good function. The forum quotes mirror real r/sauna language and will resonate with the target audience. The 'Sound familiar? That's exactly why we made this guide.' closer is a strong segue into a final CTA. Keep.
sections.issue: Minor: The closing line sets up a CTA that does not immediately follow — instead, Destinations interrupts.

sections.name: Destinations
sections.assessment: This section is not wrong in isolation — it serves SEO, builds the broader brand, and adds aspirational texture. The problem is placement. It arrives at peak conversion intent, right after Social Proof has the visitor emotionally aligned with the guide offer. Sending them to /saunas at this moment is the highest-cost distraction on the page.
sections.issue: CRITICAL: Location in the flow destroys the Social Proof-to-Final-CTA momentum arc. The Weather Channel saw a 225% conversion lift from removing distracting elements at equivalent decision points. This section should be moved or removed from the homepage entirely.

sections.name: Closing Quote
sections.assessment: The Finnish proverb is good brand expression but currently buried after Destinations. Works as a pre-CTA emotional beat. Keep, but only if it directly precedes the Final CTA.

sections.name: Final CTA
sections.assessment: Copy is solid: 'before you spend $5,000 to $25,000 on a box that gets really hot' is the most persuasive line below the hero. Well-earned position as the page closer. Keep.
sections.issue: Weakened by Destinations arriving just before it, breaking the emotional arc from Social Proof.

ctaInventory.assessment: One CTA repeated three times plus two competing CTAs. Research benchmark: pages with a single CTA convert at 13.5% vs 11.9% for pages with 2-4 CTAs. The Quiz CTA and the Destinations CTA are the two candidates for restructuring.

homeindepth.structure: Educational long-form guide with embedded calculators. Email capture via popup triggered by engagement, not inline. No quiz. Calculators create engagement without hard-gating value.
lessons: Calculators reduce friction compared to quizzes — value is immediate, email ask comes after
Popup email capture decouples the lead ask from the reading flow
No competing CTAs in the body — one consistent conversion goal

generalPatterns.homeImprovementBuyingGuides: Top-ranking pages (Lowe's, HomeDepth, FinnLeo) share one architecture: educational content builds authority, then a single lead capture at the end. None present quiz + newsletter as co-equal options on the same page.
generalPatterns.quizFunnelBestPractice: High-converting quiz funnels treat the quiz as the primary offer, with email gating of results. They do not share homepage space with a parallel newsletter signup — the quiz IS the conversion event, and email capture happens inside the quiz flow.
generalPatterns.newsletterBestPractice: Beehiiv data shows newsletter landing pages converting at 10-20% baseline, with top performers at 30-40%. They achieve this with single-goal pages, not split-goal pages.

coreConversionProblem.detail: The current page implicitly presents quiz and newsletter as equal offers. But they serve different buyer stages: the quiz suits someone ready to act (high intent), the newsletter suits someone still researching (medium intent). Mixing both on the same page without clear hierarchy means neither path is optimized. The quiz CTA is positioned before the newsletter value has been explained, which is backwards — the newsletter build-up in sections 2-6 creates the right conditions for a quiz offer, not the other way around.

quizVsNewsletterIntent.quiz: Commercial intent. Visitor wants a recommendation now. They know enough to answer questions about their setup.
quizVsNewsletterIntent.newsletter: Research intent. Visitor wants to learn before deciding. They are not ready for a specific product recommendation.
quizVsNewsletterIntent.currentPlacement: Quiz CTA appears at position 3, before the newsletter value proposition has been explained (position 4). This means the quiz is shown to visitors who are still in research mode — exactly the audience most likely to skip it.

changes.title: Remove the standalone Quiz CTA section (position 3)
changes.rationale: This section creates a competing primary CTA before the newsletter value proposition has landed. Moving or removing it does not kill the quiz — it just stops the quiz from interrupting the newsletter funnel at the worst possible moment.
changes.action: Delete the Quiz CTA section as a standalone page section. Integrate the quiz offer as a secondary option inside or below the Final CTA section, styled as a text link rather than a button: 'Not sure yet? Take the 2-minute quiz for a personalized recommendation.' This keeps the quiz alive for high-intent visitors without splitting the conversion goal.
changes.implementationNote: In page.tsx, remove the Quiz CTA section block (lines 108-129). Add a quiz text link beneath the Final CTA NewsletterSignup component.

changes.title: Move Destinations section to after the Closing Quote or remove from homepage
changes.rationale: The current placement (position 7, after Social Proof) destroys the highest-momentum point in the page. Social Proof ends with 'That's exactly why we made this guide' — a perfect handoff to the Final CTA. Destinations breaks that arc.

options.description: Delete the Destinations section from the homepage entirely. The /saunas directory is accessible via the Navigation. The homepage should stay focused on the buying guide offer.
options.tradeoff: Loses one internal link and the aspirational visual anchor. But gains full conversion focus.

options.option: B - Move to between Hero and Problem sections
options.description: Place Destinations immediately after the Hero as a brief visual 'proof of depth' moment — showing the breadth of the site — before pivoting into the problem agitation. Label it 'The best saunas in the world. Now let's find yours.' This reframes it as context-setting rather than a destination in itself.
options.tradeoff: Preserves the visual appeal. The early position means it does not interrupt the conversion arc. Risk: adds visual noise before the problem section lands.

options.option: C - Move to after Final CTA as a 'while you wait' section
options.description: Place Destinations as the last section before Footer, after the Final CTA. Frame it as exploration content for people who just signed up. Copy: 'While your first email is on its way — explore the world's best saunas.'
options.tradeoff: Best of both: conversion goal is fully completed before the visitor is offered a new destination. The section becomes a reward, not a distraction.

changes.recommendation: Option C. Moves the section to post-conversion space where it adds engagement value without competing for conversion intent.

changes.title: Rewrite Quiz CTA copy when it does appear
changes.rationale: 'Let us cut through the noise' is generic. It does not match the specificity and voice of the hero or the problem section. 'Find my sauna' is weak as a button label — it describes what the visitor wants, not what the quiz delivers.

currentCopy.headline: Let us cut through the noise.
currentCopy.subheadline: Answer 7 questions. Get a personalized recommendation with the right type, brand, budget, and installation checklist for your setup.

proposedCopy.contextNote: Use this copy when quiz appears as a secondary text link at the bottom of the Final CTA
proposedCopy.textLink: Already know your budget and space? Take the 2-minute quiz for a specific recommendation.
proposedCopy.rationale: Qualifies the visitor before they click. Only high-intent visitors self-select into the quiz. Research intent visitors stay on the newsletter path.

changes.title: Consolidate newsletter signup variants to one consistent component
changes.rationale: Three newsletter signups currently use two variants ('buying-guide-hero' and 'buying-guide'). If these render different form fields or copy, visitors who scroll past the hero may see a different value proposition at position 4 and position 9. Consistency in repeat CTAs reinforces rather than confuses.
changes.action: Audit the NewsletterSignup component to confirm all three instances render the same field set and privacy micro-copy. The headlines above each signup can differ (and should), but the form itself should be identical.

changes.title: Add a post-quiz entry point back to the newsletter
changes.rationale: If the quiz is the higher-intent path, the quiz results page should close by offering the newsletter email guide as the natural next step. This creates a loop: high-intent visitors take the quiz, quiz results offer the 3-email guide, quiz completers become newsletter subscribers. Currently these paths are parallel, not sequential.
changes.action: On the /quiz results page, add: 'Want the full breakdown of what to avoid and what to buy? The email guide covers everything in three short reads.' with a newsletter signup embedded.
changes.expectedImpact: Quiz completers are the warmest possible audience — they have already invested 2 minutes and received a recommendation. Email conversion rate on this audience should be significantly above the homepage average.

sections.name: Guide Preview (Three emails)
sections.change: moved up from position 4 — now immediately follows the problem, before authority

sections.name: Closing Quote (Finnish proverb)
sections.change: moved from position 8, now directly before Final CTA

sections.name: Final CTA
sections.change: quiz demoted from section to text link here

sections.name: Destinations (While your email arrives)
sections.change: moved from position 7 to post-conversion position

ctaInventory.assessment: Same number of CTAs, but now hierarchically clear. Newsletter is the dominant conversion goal. Quiz is a self-qualifying secondary path for high-intent visitors. Destinations is post-conversion exploration.

alternativeStructure.name: Quiz-first variant (test against recommended)
alternativeStructure.hypothesis: If the quiz converts better than the newsletter when presented as the primary offer, the entire homepage architecture should flip — quiz as primary CTA, newsletter as the email gate inside the quiz.
alternativeStructure.implementation: Make the hero CTA 'Find my sauna' (quiz). Gate quiz results behind email. Newsletter guide becomes the first email delivered. This collapses 4 CTAs into 1 while capturing higher-intent leads.
alternativeStructure.whenToTest: After implementing R1-R3 above. Run the quiz-first variant as an A/B test against the newsletter-first recommended structure. Primary metric: email captures per 1000 visitors. Secondary metric: quiz completion rate.
alternativeStructure.tradeoff: Quiz-first works better for commercial intent traffic (people searching 'best home sauna'). Newsletter-first works better for research intent traffic (people searching 'home sauna guide'). The right choice depends on which traffic source dominates — check Search Console query data to determine.

seoImpactOfRecommendations.quizCTARemoval: Zero SEO impact. The quiz is a separate page (/quiz) with its own indexing. The homepage CTA removal does not affect the quiz page's rankability.
seoImpactOfRecommendations.destinationsReposition: Slight internal link reduction from homepage to /saunas. Mitigate by ensuring /saunas is linked in the Navigation (it should already be). The /saunas directory earns its links from its own SEO, not homepage dependency.
seoImpactOfRecommendations.conversionImprovement: Higher email capture rates improve the newsletter audience quality, which supports long-term domain authority growth via branded search, social shares, and backlinks from satisfied newsletter readers.
seoImpactOfRecommendations.dwell Time: Removing the Destinations distraction may actually improve dwell time metric by keeping visitors in the conversion funnel longer rather than bouncing to /saunas and exiting.

titleTag.current: Home Sauna Buying Guide 2026 | Honest Reviews & Comparisons
titleTag.assessment: Good. Targets commercial intent ('Buying Guide 2026') and differentiates via 'Honest'. The year '2026' is current.
titleTag.recommendation: Keep. Consider testing 'Unbiased Home Sauna Buying Guide 2026' to lean harder into the independence angle that the page body emphasizes.

metaDescription.current: Find the perfect home sauna with our honest, expert-tested buying guides. Compare infrared, barrel & traditional saunas. Unbiased reviews since 2025.
metaDescription.assessment: Functional but generic. 'Expert-tested' is a claim without proof in this short space. 'Unbiased reviews since 2025' — the year is weak as a trust signal since 2025 is recent.
metaDescription.recommendation: Test: 'The buying guide written for buyers, not sellers. Real costs, honest tradeoffs, and the 12 mistakes that blow up home sauna projects. Free. No sales pitch.' This mirrors the hero copy and problem framing — visitors from search will feel they landed in the right place.

metaAndTechnicalNotes.structuredData: Verify JSON-LD is present on the homepage. Given the newsletter focus, an Organization schema with sameAs links is the minimum. If the quiz is present, a HowTo or FAQ schema targeting common questions ('how much does a home sauna cost', 'infrared vs traditional sauna') can capture featured snippets independently of the main page content.

quickWins.action: Remove standalone Quiz CTA section from page.tsx
quickWins.expectedImpact: Eliminates the primary conversion conflict. Newsletter path flows uninterrupted from Hero through Final CTA.
quickWins.risk: Low. Quiz remains accessible via Navigation and /quiz URL.

quickWins.expectedImpact: Restores Social Proof to Final CTA momentum arc. Destinations becomes an engagement layer for visitors who have already converted.

quickWins.action: Add quiz text link beneath Final CTA newsletter form
quickWins.expectedImpact: Preserves quiz discovery for high-intent visitors without competing with newsletter CTA.

quickWins.action: Update meta description to mirror hero copy
quickWins.expectedImpact: Improves click-through rate from search results by aligning SERP snippet with the page's actual tone.
